m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of :

$ echo *

Options.OptionText: It will display all files in current directory.
It will display all files and directories in current directory.
It will display all directories of current directory.

m_Item1.Explanation: echo *  will  display all files and  directories in current directory.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of command :

$ echo  '$SHELL'

Options.OptionText: It will display current user shell on screen.
It will display current username on screen.
It will display user home directory on screen.

m_Item1.Explanation: Will display $SHELL

m_Item1.QuestionText: The shell variable to print current shell is :
m_Item1.Explanation:
The shell variable to print the current shell is $SHELL. This variable contains the path to the current shell being used. The other options listed are shell variables with different purposes:

$USER: contains the username of the current user
$HOME: contains the path to the home directory of the current user
$SH: this is not a valid shell variable.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Linux , shell is  a :

Options.OptionText: Command language interpreter that executes commands.

m_Item1.Explanation: Shell is a command interpreter in Unix-Like OS.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is /bin/sh ?

Options.OptionText: Hard or symbolic link to the real shell command .
Both bash shell and bourne shell .

m_Item1.QuestionText: Linux command to accept input from user is :
m_Item1.Explanation: read is used to accept input from user. There is no data type in Linux scripts. read accepts any data.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true?

Options.OptionText: Shell is a process and can be started by superuser only.
Shell is a built-in Kernel functionality.
Shell is a wrapper for all the commands and utilities.

m_Item1.Explanation: Shell is a wrapper for all commands. It interpretes all the commands given by user. Shell resides above Kernel.
In Unix-Like systems, Shell works as command line interpreter. It interpretes and executes all the commands given by user.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a shell in Linux?

Options.OptionText: A program through which users can issue commands to Linux.
The thing that rides on the back of a turtle in Linux.

m_Item1.Explanation: Shell is command interpreter in Unix-Like OS. All the command given by user will be executed by shell.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement  is true with regards to the shell prompt?

Options.OptionText: It can be accidentally erased with backspace.

m_Item1.Explanation: Prompt in linux can be customized.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The expression expr   -9 % 2 evaluates to:
m_Item1.Explanation: % gives remainder.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is /bin/sh ?

Options.OptionText: Hard or symbolic link to the real shell command .
Both bash shell and bourne shell .

m_Item1.Explanation:
/bin/sh is the traditional Unix shell, also known as the Bourne shell. It was the original Unix shell, written by Stephen Bourne at Bell Labs. The Bourne shell is a command-line interpreter that executes commands entered by the user or read from a script. It provides a programming language with variables, control structures, and functions.

On some systems, /bin/sh may be a hard or symbolic link to another shell command, such as bash or dash, but it is intended to be compatible with the Bourne shell. The bash shell is a more modern shell that includes many features not found in the Bourne shell, but it can also be used as a replacement for the Bourne shell.

m_Item1.QuestionText: A null variable X can be created using
User requests background execution of a program by placing what at the end of the command line
m_Item1.Explanation: E

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is not a major Unix shell?
m_Item1.Explanation: The WIN shell is not a major Unix shell. It is the command-line interpreter for the Windows operating system. The C shell, bash shell, and Korn shell are all major Unix shells. The C shell was developed at the University of California, Berkeley, as a replacement for the Bourne shell. The bash shell is a more modern shell that includes many features not found in the Bourne shell. The Korn shell is a derivative of the Bourne shell that includes many features of the C shell.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The purpose of the PATH variable is to

Options.OptionText: Show the directory path of a file
Tell the shell what directories to search when a command is entered
Tell the shell in which directories new file can be created

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following represent home directory of the user ?
m_Item1.Explanation: Write explaination here..

m_Item1.QuestionText: Symbol used to redirect  the output is......
m_Item1.Explanation:
Linux provides multiple powerful symbols like >, >> and |.
Where > is a redirection symbol.
            >> is used for append.
             | is used to join the command.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command is used to copy a block of text in vi editor?
Which of the following arithmetic expression is correct?
m_Item1.Explanation:
Option A : wrong because $ to left side is wrong since $i indicates value. We cannot assign value to value . We need a variable. Hence it should be not required  i=$((i+1)).
Option B : wrong because, it requires $ at right hand side to execute a command. Hence should be i=$((i+1)).
Option C: Correct.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which is a valid statement in a shell script?
Which is a valid variable name in a shell script?
Which command is used to search for a pattern in a file in Linux Shell Programming?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'grep' command is used to search for a specified pattern within files. It stands for 'Global Regular Expression Print'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command is used to display the last 10 lines of a file in Linux by default?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'tail' command, when used without options, displays the last 10 lines of a file by default.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a shell script, how do you denote a variable?
m_Item1.Explanation: Variables in shell scripts are denoted by assigning values directly to names using the syntax: VarName=Value. No spaces are allowed around the equal sign.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can you make a shell script executable in Linux?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'chmod +x' command is used to change the file permission to make a script executable.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following symbols is used to denote the start of a comment in a bash script?
m_Item1.Explanation: In bash scripts, comments start with the '#' symbol. Any text following this symbol until the end of the line is considered a comment.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which shell is commonly considered the default shell in most Linux distributions?
m_Item1.Explanation: The Bourne-Again Shell (bash) is the default shell in most Linux distributions due to its enhanced features and wide availability.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can you check the current shell you are working in on a Linux system?
m_Item1.Explanation: The command 'echo $SHELL' prints the current shell that you are using.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which feature is exclusive to the Z Shell (zsh) compared to bash?
m_Item1.Explanation: Recursive directory expansion, which allows for the completion of a partially typed path, is a distinct feature of Z Shell (zsh).

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of the 'source' command in a shell?

Options.OptionText: To execute commands in the current shell

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'source' command causes a script to be executed in the current shell environment, without starting a new process.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary configuration file for bash shell settings?
m_Item1.Explanation: The '~/.bashrc' file is used to configure user-specific aliases and functions that are loaded whenever a terminal session is started in interactive mode.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following Linux shells is known for its improvements over the older Bourne Shell?
m_Item1.Explanation: Bash (Bourne Again SHell) is an enhanced version of the original Bourne Shell and includes a number of improvements such as command-line editing.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which shell is primarily associated with programming and scripting, particularly among users of the BSD operating systems?
m_Item1.Explanation: The C Shell (csh) is known for its syntax, which is similar to C programming, and is predominantly used in BSD systems.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which shell is known for its powerful scripting abilities and resembles the features of both the C Shell and the Bourne Shell?
m_Item1.Explanation: Korn Shell (ksh) offers advanced scripting features and combines elements from both the C Shell and the Bourne Shell.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which shell is particularly recognized for its user-friendly interactive features, such as autosuggestions and syntax highlighting?
m_Item1.Explanation: Fish Shell (Friendly Interactive SHell) is known for its easy-to-use interactive features that enhance user experience.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What command is used in BASH to display the value of a variable named 'USER'?
m_Item1.Explanation: In BASH, the 'echo' command is used to display the value of a variable. '$USER' is the correct way to refer to the value of the variable named 'USER'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following commands is used to give execute permissions to a script file in BASH?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'chmod +x' command is used to add execute permissions to a file, allowing it to be run as a script.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the '!' operator do in a BASH conditional statement?

Options.OptionText: It is used to redirect output
It is a logical OR operator

m_Item1.Explanation: In BASH, the '!' operator negates the result of the condition that follows it.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command in BASH is used to terminate a running process with the provided Process ID (PID)?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'kill' command in BASH is used to send a signal to a process, which can terminate it. Providing the PID with 'kill' will terminate that particular process.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the significance of the 'source' command in BASH?

Options.OptionText: Compiles a script into machine code
Executes a script in a new shell
Runs a script in the current shell context
Converts a script into a binary executable

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'source' command executes a script within the current shell, meaning it allows changes to environment variables within the script to affect the current shell.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which symbol is used to represent the home directory in a BASH script?
m_Item1.Explanation: The tilde symbol '~' is used in BASH to represent the home directory of the current user.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the command 'set -x' do when debugging a BASH script?

Options.OptionText: Prints commands and their arguments as they are executed

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'set -x' command enables a mode of the shell where all executed commands are printed to the terminal with their arguments, which helps in debugging.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In BASH, what is the effect of using '&&' between two commands?

Options.OptionText: Runs both commands at the same time
Runs the second command only if the first fails
Runs the second command only if the first succeeds
Combines the output of both commands

m_Item1.Explanation: In BASH, using '&&' between two commands ensures that the second command is executed only if the first command is successful (returns an exit status of 0).

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the main difference between environment variables and user-defined variables in Linux?

Options.OptionText: Environment variables are only available to the user who created them.
User-defined variables are persistent across sessions, unlike environment variables.
Environment variables are inherited by child processes, but user-defined variables are not.
User-defined variables can only store numerical values.

m_Item1.Explanation: Environment variables are available to child processes, making them useful for passing information beyond the current shell. User-defined variables are local to the shell in which they are created.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following commands would correctly export a user-defined variable to become an environment variable?
m_Item1.Explanation: To export a shell variable so it becomes an environment variable, the 'export' command is used, followed by the variable name and assignment.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of the .bashrc file in a Linux system?

Options.OptionText: To configure the system's kernel modules
To customize user-specific shell environments upon startup

m_Item1.Explanation: .bashrc is a script that is executed whenever a new terminal session is opened in interactive mode. It's used to configure the user's environment with custom settings like aliases, environment variables, and functions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command can you use to immediately apply changes made in the .bashrc file without logging out and back in?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'source' command is used to execute the content of a script in the current shell environment. By running 'source ~/.bashrc', you apply changes made to the .bashrc file instantly.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In which directory is the .bashrc file typically located?
m_Item1.Explanation: The .bashrc file is a user configuration file and is located in the user's home directory, represented by '~/'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if you delete your .bashrc file and start a new terminal session?

Options.OptionText: The terminal will fail to open
The terminal will use default shell settings
The system will automatically create a new .bashrc with default settings
The previous .bashrc file will be restored from a backup

m_Item1.Explanation: If the .bashrc file is deleted, the terminal will revert to using the default shell settings as there is no custom configuration for the user's shell environment.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of the .profile file in a Linux system?

Options.OptionText: To configure the system's network settings
To set environment variables and startup programs for a user session

m_Item1.Explanation: The .profile file in a Linux system is used to set environment variables and define startup programs when a user logs in. It helps customize the user's shell environment.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which shell primarily uses the .profile file for configuration settings?
m_Item1.Explanation: The .profile file is used by the Bourne shell (sh) and its derivatives for setting up a user's environment at login.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Where is the .profile file typically located for a user in a Linux system?
m_Item1.Explanation: The .profile file is usually located in the user's home directory, i.e., /home/user/.profile, and is used to configure environment settings specific to that user.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If a system has both .profile and .bash_profile files, which one will be executed by the bash shell upon login?
m_Item1.Explanation: When using the bash shell, if both .bash_profile and .profile files are present, bash will prioritize and execute the .bash_profile file upon login.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of the .bash_profile file in Linux?

Options.OptionText: To store the user's commands history.
To store user-specific environment variables and startup scripts.
To manage network configurations for the user.

m_Item1.Explanation: .bash_profile is a script that is executed for login shells and is used to store user-specific environment variables and startup scripts.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Where is the .bash_profile file typically located for a user?
m_Item1.Explanation: The .bash_profile file is typically located in the user's home directory, denoted as $HOME.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command can be used to immediately apply changes made to the .bash_profile file for the current session?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'source' command is used to apply changes made to .bash_profile in the current shell session without logging out and back in.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If both .bashrc and .bash_profile files exist, which one is executed by default when a user logs in through a terminal?

Options.OptionText: Neither file is executed by default.

m_Item1.Explanation: .bash_profile is executed for login shells, while .bashrc is typically executed for non-login shells.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of the .bash_logout file in a user's home directory?

Options.OptionText: To log user activity during a bash session
To execute commands when the user logs out of a bash session

m_Item1.Explanation: .bash_logout is used to execute commands when a user logs out of a bash session, allowing for cleanup or any final tasks to be performed before exiting.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command can you typically add to the .bash_logout file to clear the screen upon logout?
m_Item1.Explanation: Adding the 'clear' command to the .bash_logout file will clear the terminal screen when the user logs out, providing a clean start for the next session.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Where is the .bash_logout file typically located?
m_Item1.Explanation: The .bash_logout file is typically located in the user's home directory ($HOME), where it executes specific commands upon the user's logout from the shell session.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command is used in a shell script to check if a file exists?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'test -f' command in a shell script is used to check if a file exists and is a regular file.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the '$#' variable represent in a shell script?

Options.OptionText: The exit status of the last command
The path to the shell binary
The number of arguments passed to the script

m_Item1.Explanation: '$#' is a special variable in shell scripts that stores the number of arguments passed to the script.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a shell script, which symbol is used to define a comment?
m_Item1.Explanation: In shell scripts, the '#' symbol is used to define a comment. Everything after it on the same line is ignored by the shell.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can you make a shell script executable?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'chmod +x' command is used to add the executable permission to a shell script, allowing it to be run as a standalone program.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command is used to print text to the terminal in a shell script?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'echo' command is used in shell scripts to print text or variables to the terminal.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a shell script, which wildcard character is used to represent zero or more characters in a filename pattern?
m_Item1.Explanation: The asterisk (*) wildcard character is used in shell scripts to match zero or more characters in a filename pattern.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When using wildcards in shell scripts, what does the question mark (?) represent?
m_Item1.Explanation: In shell scripts, the question mark (?) is a wildcard that represents any single character in a filename.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which command using wildcards will list all files with a .txt extension in the current directory?
m_Item1.Explanation: The command 'ls *.txt' uses the asterisk (*) wildcard to match any file ending in .txt within the current directory.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If you want to list all files starting with 'a', followed by any two characters, and ending with '.sh', which wildcard pattern would you use?
m_Item1.Explanation: The pattern 'a??.sh' matches files starting with 'a', followed by exactly two characters (using ?), and ending with '.sh'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: You have files named file1.txt, file2.txt, and fileA.txt. Which wildcard pattern will match only file1.txt and file2.txt?
m_Item1.Explanation: The pattern 'file[1-2].txt' matches filenames that start with 'file', followed by the characters '1' or '2', and end with '.txt'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What special variable is used to represent all the command line arguments as a single word in a shell script?
m_Item1.Explanation: The special variable $* represents all command line arguments as a single word, whereas $@ treats them as separate words.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which special variable holds the number of command line arguments passed to a shell script?
m_Item1.Explanation: The special variable $# gives the total number of arguments passed to the script.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a shell script, how would you access the second command line argument passed?
m_Item1.Explanation: Command line arguments are accessed using $1, $2, $3, etc. The variable $2 specifically accesses the second command line argument.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the difference between using $* and $@ in a shell script when enclosing them in double quotes?

Options.OptionText: They behave the same way, listing all arguments separated by spaces.
$@ behaves like a single string, while $* treats each argument as separate.
$* concatenates all arguments into a single argument, while $@ treats them as separate arguments.
$* and $@ cannot be used in double quotes.

m_Item1.Explanation: When enclosed in double quotes, "$*" treats all arguments as a single string, while "$@" treats each argument as a separate string.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of the 'read' command in a shell script?

Options.OptionText: It writes output to the console.
It reads input from the user.

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'read' command in a shell script is used to read input from the user or from a file. It assigns the input to a variable, allowing the script to use the input further in the command execution.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true about the 'echo' command in shell scripts?

Options.OptionText: It reads input from the user.
It is used to output text to the terminal.
It deletes files in the directory.
It compiles programs written in C.

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'echo' command is used in shell scripts to display text or variables to the terminal. It is a simple way to output messages or the values of variables to the console.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can you use the 'read' command to prompt for input with a custom message?
m_Item1.Explanation: You can use the '-p' option with the 'read' command to display a prompt message when asking for input. The syntax is 'read -p "message" variable', where 'message' is the prompt and 'variable' is where the input will be stored.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will the command 'echo $SHELL' output in a shell script?

Options.OptionText: The exit status of the last command

m_Item1.Explanation: The '$SHELL' variable contains the path of the shell that is currently being used, so 'echo $SHELL' will output the name of the executing shell.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following 'read' command options can be used to not end with a newline after displaying the prompt?
m_Item1.Explanation: The '-p' option in the 'read' command is used to display a prompt without moving to a newline, allowing the input to be collected directly after the prompt.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of the 'tput' command in Linux?

Options.OptionText: To initialize and control terminal settings

m_Item1.Explanation: 'tput' is used to initialize terminals and control various terminal attributes, like colors and cursor position.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which 'tput' subcommand is used to set the foreground text color of a terminal?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'setaf' subcommand of 'tput' is used to set the foreground color in the terminal.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the 'tput sgr0' command do?

Options.OptionText: Resets all terminal attributes to their default settings

m_Item1.Explanation: 'tput sgr0' is used to reset all attributes like colors and boldness to their default terminal settings.
